key_insight: The paper turns legal reasoning into a trainable reward signal by pairing LLM-based autoformalization with a formal verifier, so legal AI can learn from externally checkable outcomes.
engineering_relevance: For legal AI systems, this offers a path to safer training and deployment by enforcing verifiable steps, valid deductions, and correctness for computational legal tasks.
method: Autoformalize legal problems into a formal calculus extending Catala.
Use a verification kernel to check proof traces and compute reward signals.
Generate explanations directly from formal proof traces to make reasoning auditable.
evidence_signal: The architecture is demonstrated on three legal case types: German procedural deadlines, U.S. Commerce Clause analysis, and cross-jurisdictional sanction proportionality.
watch_item: Open-textured legal judgment still depends on formal coverage of argument stages, so real-world performance may be limited by how completely the formal calculus captures doctrine.
paper_title: Closing the Loop: Formally Verified Law as a Reward Signal for Self-Improving Legal AI
